šuttu kīma imbāri inanappaḥšu
title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI: The Flood
author: Sin-leqi-unninni

en: In the beginning was the Word (Logos, λόγος), and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
orig: ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος · καὶ ὁ λόγος ἦν πρὸς τὸν θεόν · καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: He was in the beginning with God.
orig: οὗτος ἦν ἐν ἀρχῇ πρὸς τὸν θεόν ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: All things came into being through him, and apart from him not one thing came into being that has come into being.
orig: πάντα δι’ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο · καὶ χωρὶς αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο οὐδὲ ἕν · ὃ γέγονεν
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: In him was life, and the life was the light of humankind. And the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness did not overcome it.
orig: ἐν αὐτῷ ζωὴ ἦν · καὶ ἡ ζωὴ ἦν τὸ φῶς τῶν ἀνθρώπων · καὶ τὸ φῶς ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ φαίνει · καὶ ἡ σκοτία αὐτὸ οὐ κατέλαβεν ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: There was a man sent from God, whose name was John. He came as a witness, to testify concerning the light, so that all might believe through him. He was not the light, but came to testify concerning the light.
orig: ἐγένετο ἄνθρωπος ἀπεσταλμένος παρὰ θεοῦ · ὄνομα αὐτῷ Ἰωάννης · οὗτος ἦλθεν εἰς μαρτυρίαν · ἵνα μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ φωτός · ἵνα πάντες πιστεύσωσιν δι’ αὐτοῦ · οὐκ ἦν ἐκεῖνος τὸ φῶς · ἀλλ’ ἵνα μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ φωτός ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: The true light, which enlightens every person, was coming into the world. He was in the world, and the world came into being through him, yet the world did not know him. He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him.
orig: ἦν τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινόν · ὃ φωτίζει πάντα ἄνθρωπον ἐρχόμενον εἰς τὸν κόσμον · ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἦν · καὶ ὁ κόσμος δι’ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο · καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτὸν οὐκ ἔγνω · εἰς τὰ ἴδια ἦλθεν · καὶ οἱ ἴδιοι αὐτὸν οὐ παρέλαβον ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: But to all who received him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God, who were born not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of a man, but of God.
orig: ὅσοι δὲ ἔλαβον αὐτόν · ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ἐξουσίαν τέκνα θεοῦ γενέσθαι · τοῖς πιστεύουσιν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ · οἳ οὐκ ἐξ αἱμάτων · οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος σαρκὸς · οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος ἀνδρὸς · ἀλλ’ ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, glory as of the only-begotten (monogenes, μονογενής) from the Father, full of grace and truth.
orig: καὶ ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο · καὶ ἐσκήνωσεν ἐν ἡμῖν · καὶ ἐθεασάμεθα τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ · δόξαν ὡς μονογενοῦς παρὰ πατρός · πλήρης χάριτος καὶ ἀληθείας ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: John testified concerning him and cried out, saying, “This was the one of whom I said: the one coming after me has surpassed me, because he was before me.” For from his fullness (pleroma, πλήρωμα) we have all received, grace upon grace.
orig: Ἰωάννης μαρτυρεῖ περὶ αὐτοῦ · καὶ κέκραγεν λέγων · οὗτος ἦν ὃν εἶπον · ὁ ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν · ὅτι πρῶτός μου ἦν · καὶ ἐκ τοῦ πληρώματος αὐτοῦ ἡμεῖς πάντες ἐλάβομεν · καὶ χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ. No one has ever seen God. The only-begotten God (monogenes theos, μονογενὴς θεός), the one who is in the bosom of the Father, he has made him known.
orig: ὅτι ὁ νόμος διὰ Μωυσέως ἐδόθη · ἡ χάρις καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐγένετο · θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε · μονογενὴς θεὸς ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρὸς · ἐκεῖνος ἐξηγήσατο ·
title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)

en: Then came the Festival of Dedication in Jerusalem. It was winter, and Jesus was walking in the temple, in the Portico of Solomon. The Jews gathered around him and said to him, “How long will you keep us in suspense? If you are the Christ, tell us plainly.” Jesus answered them, “I told you, and you do not believe. The works that I do in the name of my Father, these testify concerning me. But you do not believe, because you are not among my sheep.”
orig: ἐγένετο τότε τὰ ἐνκαίνια ἐν τοῖς Ἱεροσολύμοις · χειμὼν ἦν · καὶ περιεπάτει ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ ἐν τῇ στοᾷ τοῦ Σολομῶνος · ἐκύκλωσαν οὖν αὐτὸν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ ἔλεγον αὐτῷ · ἕως πότε τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν αἴρεις · εἰ σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός · εἰπὲ ἡμῖν παρρησίᾳ ·
title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
author: John (attributed)

en: “My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish, and no one shall snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the hand of the Father. I and the Father are one.”
orig: τὰ πρόβατα τὰ ἐμὰ τῆς φωνῆς μου ἀκούουσιν · κἀγὼ γινώσκω αὐτὰ καὶ ἀκολουθοῦσιν μοι · κἀγὼ δίδωμι αὐτοῖς ζωὴν αἰώνιον · καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀπόλωνται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα · καὶ οὐχ ἁρπάσει τις αὐτὰ ἐκ τῆς χειρός μου · ὁ πατήρ μου ὃ δέδωκέν μοι πάντων μεῖζόν ἐστιν · καὶ οὐδεὶς δύναται ἁρπάζειν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς τοῦ πατρός · ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν ·
title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
author: John (attributed)

en:
The Jews took up stones again to stone him. Jesus answered them, “I have shown you many good works from the Father. For which of these works are you going to stone me?” The Jews answered him, “It is not for a good work that we are going to stone you, but for blasphemy, because you, being a man, make yourself God.”
        Jesus answered them, “Is it not written in your Law, ‘I said, you are gods’? If he called them gods to whom the word of God came — and scripture cannot be broken — do you say of the one whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world, ‘You are blaspheming,’ because I said, ‘I am the Son of God’?”
orig: ἐβάστασαν πάλιν λίθους οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἵνα λιθάσωσιν αὐτόν · ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς · πολλὰ ἔργα καλὰ ἔδειξα ὑμῖν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός · διὰ ποῖον αὐτῶν ἔργον ἐμὲ λιθάζετε · ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι · περὶ καλοῦ ἔργου οὐ λιθάζομέν σε ἀλλὰ περὶ βλασφημίας · καὶ ὅτι σὺ ἄνθρωπος ὢν ποιεῖς σεαυτὸν θεόν · ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς · οὐκ ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν · ἐγὼ εἶπα θεοί ἐστε · εἰ ἐκείνους εἶπεν θεοὺς πρὸς οὓς ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐγένετο · καὶ οὐ δύναται λυθῆναι ἡ γραφή · ὃν ὁ πατὴρ ἡγίασεν καὶ ἀπέστειλεν εἰς τὸν κόσμον · ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι βλασφημεῖς · ὅτι εἶπον υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ εἰμι ·
title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
author: John (attributed)

en: “If I am not doing the works of my Father, do not believe me. But if I do them, even though you do not believe me, believe the works, so that you may know and understand that the Father is in me and I am in the Father.” They tried again to seize him, but he escaped from their hands.
orig: εἰ οὐ ποιῶ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρός μου · μὴ πιστεύετέ μοι · εἰ δὲ ποιῶ · κἂν ἐμοὶ μὴ πιστεύητε · τοῖς ἔργοις πιστεύετε · ἵνα γνῶτε καὶ γινώσκητε ὅτι ἐν ἐμοὶ ὁ πατὴρ κἀγὼ ἐν τῷ πατρί · ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πάλιν πιάσαι · καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς αὐτῶν ·
title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
author: John (attributed)

en: His divine power has granted to us all things that pertain to life and godliness, through the knowledge (epignosis, ἐπίγνωσις) of him who called us by his own glory and excellence,
orig: ὡς πάντα ἡμῖν τῆς θείας δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ τὰ πρὸς ζωὴν καὶ εὐσέβειαν δεδωρημένης · διὰ τῆς ἐπιγνώσεως τοῦ καλέσαντος ἡμᾶς ἰδίᾳ δόξῃ καὶ ἀρετῇ ·
title: 2 Peter 1:3–4: “Partakers of the Divine Nature”
author: Peter (attributed)

en: by which he has granted to us the precious and very great promises, so that through them you may become partakers of the divine nature (θείας κοινωνοὶ φύσεως), having escaped the corruption that is in the world because of sinful desire.
orig: δι’ ὧν τὰ τίμια καὶ μέγιστα ἡμῖν ἐπαγγέλματα δεδώρηται · ἵνα διὰ τούτων γένησθε θείας κοινωνοὶ φύσεως · ἀποφυγόντες τῆς ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἐν ἐπιθυμίᾳ φθορᾶς ·
title: 2 Peter 1:3–4: “Partakers of the Divine Nature”
author: Peter (attributed)

en: For he was made man that we might be made God (θεοποιηθῶμεν); and he manifested himself through a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father; and he endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality.
orig: Αὐτὸς γὰρ ἐνηνθρώπησεν, ἵνα ἡμεῖς θεοποιηθῶμεν· καὶ ἐφανέρωσεν ἑαυτὸν διὰ σώματος, ἵνα ἡμεῖς τοῦ ἀοράτου Πατρὸς ἔννοιαν λάβωμεν· καὶ ὑπέμεινε τὴν παρὰ ἀνθρώπων ὕβριν, ἵνα ἡμεῖς ἀφθαρσίαν κληρονομήσωμεν.
title: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54: “He Became Man That We Might Become God”
author: Athanasius of Alexandria

en: For he did not suffer any loss from being in a body, nor was he defiled by being in it, but rather he deified (ἐθεοποίησεν) what he put on, and beyond this, bestowed this gift bountifully upon the race of men.
orig: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἐζημιοῦτο ἐν σώματι ὠν, οὐδὲ ἐμολύνετο ἐν αὐτῷ ὠν, ἀλλὰ μᾶλλον ἐθεοποίησε τὸ φορεθέν, καὶ πλέον καὶ τῷ γένει τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἐχαρίσατο τοῦτο.
title: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54: “He Became Man That We Might Become God”
author: Athanasius of Alexandria

en: I do not ask on behalf of these alone, but also on behalf of those who believe in me through their word, that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you sent me.
orig: οὐ περὶ τούτων δὲ ἐρωτῶ μόνον · ἀλλὰ καὶ περὶ τῶν πιστευόντων διὰ τοῦ λόγου αὐτῶν εἰς ἐμέ · ἵνα πάντες ἓν ὦσιν · καθὼς σύ πάτερ ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν σοί · ἵνα καὶ αὐτοὶ ἐν ἡμῖν ὦσιν · ἵνα ὁ κόσμος πιστεύῃ ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας ·
title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
author: John (attributed)

en: And the glory (doxa, δόξα) which you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one just as we are one—I in them and you in me—that they may be perfected into one, so that the world may know that you sent me, and that you loved them just as you loved me.
orig: κἀγὼ τὴν δόξαν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς · ἵνα ὦσιν ἓν καθὼς ἡμεῖς ἕν · ἐγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς καὶ σὺ ἐν ἐμοί · ἵνα ὦσιν τετελειωμένοι εἰς ἕν · ἵνα γινώσκῃ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας · καὶ ἠγάπησας αὐτοὺς καθὼς ἐμὲ ἠγάπησας ·
title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
author: John (attributed)

en: Father, I desire that those also whom you have given me may be with me where I am, so that they may see my glory which you have given me, because you loved me before the foundation of the world. Righteous Father, the world did not know you, but I knew you, and these knew that you sent me. And I made known to them your name, and I will make it known, so that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them.
orig: πάτερ · ὃ δέδωκάς μοι · θέλω ἵνα ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ κἀκεῖνοι ὦσιν μετ’ ἐμοῦ · ἵνα θεωρῶσιν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἐμὴν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι · ὅτι ἠγάπησάς με πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου · πάτερ δίκαιε · καὶ ὁ κόσμος σε οὐκ ἔγνω · ἐγὼ δέ σε ἔγνων · καὶ οὗτοι ἔγνωσαν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας · καὶ ἐγνώρισα αὐτοῖς τὸ ὄνομά σου · καὶ γνωρίσω · ἵνα ἡ ἀγάπη ἣν ἠγάπησάς με ἐν αὐτοῖς ᾖ · κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς ·
title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
author: John (attributed)

en: Since many have undertaken to compile a narrative concerning the events that have been fulfilled among us, just as those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word (autoptai kai hyperetai tou logou, αὐτόπται καὶ ὑπηρέται τοῦ λόγου) delivered them to us,
orig: ἐπειδήπερ πολλοὶ ἐπεχείρησαν ἀνατάξασθαι διήγησιν περὶ τῶν πεπληροφορημένων ἐν ἡμῖν πραγμάτων · καθὼς παρέδοσαν ἡμῖν οἱ ἀπ’ ἀρχῆς αὐτόπται καὶ ὑπηρέται γενόμενοι τοῦ λόγου ·
title: Gospel of Luke 1:1-4: The Historian's Prologue
author: Luke (attributed)

en: it seemed good to me also, having followed all things closely from the beginning, to write for you an orderly account, most excellent Theophilus, so that you may know the certainty (asphaleia, ἀσφάλεια) of the things about which you have been instructed.
orig: ἔδοξεν κἀμοὶ παρηκολουθηκότι ἄνωθεν πᾶσιν ἀκριβῶς καθεξῆς σοι γράψαι · κράτιστε Θεόφιλε · ἵνα ἐπιγνῷς περὶ ὧν κατηχήθης λόγων τὴν ἀσφάλειαν ·
title: Gospel of Luke 1:1-4: The Historian's Prologue
author: Luke (attributed)

en: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, Son of God. As it is written in Isaiah the prophet: Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way; the voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.
orig: Ἀρχὴ τοῦ εὐαγγελίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ υἱοῦ θεοῦ · καθὼς γέγραπται ἐν τῷ Ἠσαΐᾳ τῷ προφήτῃ · ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου πρὸ προσώπου σου · ὃς κατασκευάσει τὴν ὁδόν σου · φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ · ἑτοιμάσατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου · εὐθείας ποιεῖτε τὰς τρίβους αὐτοῦ ·
title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
author: Mark (attributed)

en: John appeared, baptizing in the wilderness and proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. And all the country of Judea and all the people of Jerusalem were going out to him and were being baptized by him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins. And John was clothed with camel’s hair and wore a leather belt around his waist, and ate locusts and wild honey.
orig: ἐγένετο Ἰωάννης βαπτίζων ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ κηρύσσων βάπτισμα μετανοίας εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν ·
title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
author: Mark (attributed)

en: And he proclaimed, saying: After me comes the one who is mightier than I, the strap of whose sandals I am not worthy to stoop down and untie. I have baptized you with water, but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.
orig: καὶ ἐκήρυσσεν λέγων · ἔρχεται ὁ ἰσχυρότερός μου ὀπίσω μου · οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἱκανὸς κύψας λῦσαι τὸν ἱμάντα τῶν ὑποδημάτων αὐτοῦ · ἐγὼ ἐβάπτισα ὑμᾶς ὕδατι · αὐτὸς δὲ βαπτίσει ὑμᾶς πνεύματι ἁγίῳ ·
title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
author: Mark (attributed)

en: In those days Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee and was baptized in the Jordan by John. And immediately, coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens being torn apart (schizomenous, σχιζομένους) and the Spirit descending into him like a dove. And a voice came from the heavens: You are my beloved Son; in you I am well pleased.
orig: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν ἐκείναις ταῖς ἡμέραις ἦλθεν Ἰησοῦς ἀπὸ Ναζαρὲτ τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ ἐβαπτίσθη εἰς τὸν Ἰορδάνην ὑπὸ Ἰωάννου · καὶ εὐθὺς ἀναβαίνων ἐκ τοῦ ὕδατος εἶδεν σχιζομένους τοὺς οὐρανοὺς καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα ὡς περιστερὰν καταβαῖνον εἰς αὐτόν · καὶ φωνὴ ἐγένετο ἐκ τῶν οὐρανῶν · σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός · ἐν σοὶ εὐδόκησα ·
title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
author: Mark (attributed)

en: You have heard that it was said, ‘You shall love your neighbour and hate your enemy.’ But I say to you: Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, so that you may become sons of your Father who is in the heavens, for he makes his sun rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the just and the unjust.
orig: Ἠκούσατε ὅτι ἐρρέθη · ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου καὶ μισήσεις τὸν ἐχθρόν σου · ἐγὼ δὲ λέγω ὑμῖν · ἀγαπᾶτε τοὺς ἐχθροὺς ὑμῶν καὶ προσεύχεσθε ὑπὲρ τῶν διωκόντων ὑμᾶς · ὅπως γένησθε υἱοὶ τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν τοῦ ἐν οὐρανοῖς · ὅτι τὸν ἥλιον αὐτοῦ ἀνατέλλει ἐπὶ πονηροὺς καὶ ἀγαθούς · καὶ βρέχει ἐπὶ δικαίους καὶ ἀδίκους ·
title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: For if you love those who love you, what reward do you have? Do not even the tax collectors do the same? And if you greet only your brothers, what more are you doing than others? Do not even the gentiles do the same?
orig: ἐὰν γὰρ ἀγαπήσητε τοὺς ἀγαπῶντας ὑμᾶς · τίνα μισθὸν ἔχετε · οὐχὶ καὶ οἱ τελῶναι τὸ αὐτὸ ποιοῦσιν · καὶ ἐὰν ἀσπάσησθε τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς ὑμῶν μόνον · τί περισσὸν ποιεῖτε · οὐχὶ καὶ οἱ ἐθνικοὶ τὸ αὐτὸ ποιοῦσιν ·
title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: Be perfect (τέλειοι, teleioi), therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.
orig: ἔσεσθε οὖν ὑμεῖς τέλειοι · ὡς ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν ὁ οὐράνιος τέλειός ἐστιν ·
title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: For this is why the Word of God is man, and this is why the Son of God became the Son of Man: so that man, having been taken into the Word and receiving adoption, might become a son of God. For we could in no other way have attained to incorruptibility and immortality, unless we had been united to incorruptibility and immortality. But how could we be united to incorruptibility and immortality, unless first incorruptibility and immortality had become what we are, so that the corruptible might be swallowed up by incorruptibility, and the mortal by immortality, so that we might receive the adoption as sons?
orig: Propter hoc enim Verbum Dei homo, et qui Filius Dei est Filius hominis factus est, commixtus Verbo Dei, ut adoptionem percipiens fiat filius Dei. Non enim poteramus aliter incorruptelam et immortalitatem percipere, nisi aduniti fuissemus incorruptelae et immortalitati. Quemadmodum autem aduniri possemus incorruptelae et immortalitati, nisi prius incorruptela et immortalitas facta fuisset id quod et nos, ut absorberetur quod erat corruptibile ab incorruptela, et quod erat mortale ab immortalitate, ut adoptionem filiorum perciperemus?
title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
author: Irenaeus of Lyon

en: For the Word of God, our Lord Jesus Christ, who through his transcendent love became what we are, that he might bring us to be even what he is himself.
orig: Verbum Dei, Dominus noster Iesus Christus, qui propter immensam suam dilectionem factus est quod sumus nos, uti nos perficeret esse quod est ipse.
title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
author: Irenaeus of Lyon

en: For if he was not made man, he could not have been received as a man. And if he did not receive the condition of man, he could not, being man, have overcome that which is hostile to man. And what he overcome he conquered justly; and he also gave what he gave as God, freely and by grace. And again, unless man had been joined to God, he could never have become a partaker of incorruptibility. For it was incumbent upon the Mediator between God and men, by his relationship to both, to bring both to friendship and concord, and to present man to God, and to reveal God to man.
orig: Si enim non homo factus esset, non potuisset ut homo recipi. Et si non conditionem hominis recepisset, non potuisset, homo exsistens, quod adversum est homini vincere. Et quod vicit, iuste vicit; et quod dedit, ut Deus dedit, liberaliter et per gratiam. Rursus nisi homo Deo coniunctus fuisset, non potuisset particeps fieri incorruptelae. Oportebat enim Mediatorem Dei et hominum, per suam ad utrosque domesticitatem, in amicitiam et concordiam utrosque reducere, et Deo quidem sistere hominem, homini autem notum facere Deum.
title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
author: Irenaeus of Lyon

en: God stands in the assembly of gods; in the midst of gods he renders judgment.
orig: Ἁ θεὸς ἔστη ἐν συναγωγῇ θεῶν· ἐν μέσῳ δὲ θεοὺς διακρινεῖ.
title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)

en: How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to sinners? Give justice to the weak and the orphan; vindicate the afflicted and the destitute. Rescue the weak and the needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked.
orig: ἕως πότε κρίνετε ἀδικίαν καὶ πρόσωπα ἁμαρτωλῶν λαμβάνετε; κρίνατε ὀρφανὸν καὶ πτωχόν· ταπεινὸν καὶ πένητα δικαιώσατε. ἐξέλεσθε πένητα καὶ πτωχόν· ἐκ χειρὸς ἁμαρτωλοῦ ῥύσασθε.
title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)

en: They have neither knowledge nor understanding; they walk about in darkness. All the foundations of the earth are shaken.
orig: οὐκ ἔγνωσαν οὐδὲ συνῆκαν· ἐν σκότει διαπορεύονται· σαλευθήσονται πάντα τὰ θεμέλια τῆς γῆς.
title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)

en: I said, “You are gods, and all of you are sons of the Most High. But you shall die like mortals, and fall like one of the princes.”
orig: ἐγὼ εἶπα· θεοί ἐστε καὶ υἱοὶ Ὑψίστου πάντες· ὑμεῖς δὲ ὡς ἄνθρωποι ἀποθνῃσκετε καὶ ὡς εἷς τῶν ἀρχόντων πίπτετε.
title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)

en: Rise up, O God, judge the earth; for you shall inherit all the nations.
orig: ἀνάστα, ὁ θεός, κρῖνον τὴν γῆν· ὅτι σὺ κατακληρονομήσεις ἐν πᾶσιν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν.
title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)

en: And God said, “Let us make humankind according to our image (eikon, εἰκών) and according to our likeness (homoiosis, ὁμοίωσις), and let them rule over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heaven and over the cattle and over all the earth and over every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth.” And God made humankind; according to the image of God he made him; male and female he made them.
orig: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ θεός· ποιήσωμεν ἄνθρωπον κατ’ εἰκόνα ἡμετέραν καὶ καθ’ ὁμοίωσιν, καὶ ἀρχέτωσαν τῶν ἰχθύων τῆς θαλάσσης καὶ τῶν πετεινῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ τῶν κτηνῶν καὶ πάσης τῆς γῆς καὶ πάντων τῶν ἑρπετῶν τῶν ἑρπόντων ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς. καὶ ἐποίησεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν ἄνθρωπον· κατ’ εἰκόνα θεοῦ ἐποίησεν αὐτόν· ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ἐποίησεν αὐτούς.
title: Genesis 1:26-28: "Let Us Make Man in Our Image"
author: Moses (attributed)

en: And God blessed them, saying, “Increase and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and rule over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heaven and over all the cattle and over all the earth and over all the creeping things that creep upon the earth.”
orig: καὶ ηὐλόγησεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεὸς λέγων· αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε καὶ πληρώσατε τὴν γῆν καὶ κατακυριεύσατε αὐτῆς καὶ ἄρχετε τῶν ἰχθύων τῆς θαλάσσης καὶ τῶν πετεινῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ πάντων τῶν κτηνῶν καὶ πάσης τῆς γῆς καὶ πάντων τῶν ἑρπετῶν τῶν ἑρπόντων ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς.
title: Genesis 1:26-28: "Let Us Make Man in Our Image"
author: Moses (attributed)

en: We believe in one God, the Father, the Almighty, maker of heaven and earth, of all things visible and invisible.
orig: Πιστεύομεν εἰς ἕνα Θεόν, Πατέρα, Παντοκράτορα, ποιητὴν οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς, ὁρατῶν τε πάντων καὶ ἀοράτων.
title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
author: Council of Nicaea / Constantinople

en: And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the only-begotten Son of God, begotten of the Father before all ages; light from light, true God from true God; begotten, not made, of one substance (homoousios, ὁμοούσιος) with the Father, through whom all things were made. Who for us humans and for our salvation came down from the heavens and was incarnate of the Holy Spirit and the Virgin Mary, and became man. He was crucified for us under Pontius Pilate, and suffered, and was buried. And on the third day he rose again according to the Scriptures. And ascended into the heavens and sits at the right hand of the Father. And he shall come again with glory to judge the living and the dead, of whose kingdom there shall be no end.
orig: Καὶ εἰς ἕνα Κύριον Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν, τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ Θεοῦ τὸν μονογενῆ, τὸν ἐκ τοῦ Πατρὸς γεννηθέντα πρὸ πάντων τῶν αἰώνων· φῶς ἐκ φωτός, Θεὸν ἀληθινὸν ἐκ Θεοῦ ἀληθινοῦ· γεννηθέντα, οὐ ποιηθέντα, ὁμοούσιον τῷ Πατρί, δι’ οὗ τὰ πάντα ἐγένετο· τὸν δι’ ἡμᾶς τοὺς ἀνθρώπους καὶ διὰ τὴν ἡμετέραν σωτηρίαν κατελθόντα ἐκ τῶν οὐρανῶν καὶ σαρκωθέντα ἐκ Πνεύματος Ἁγίου καὶ Μαρίας τῆς Παρθένου καὶ ἐνανθρωπήσαντα· σταυρωθέντα τε ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν ἐπὶ Ποντίου Πιλάτου καὶ παθόντα καὶ ταφέντα· καὶ ἀναστάντα τῇ τρίτῃ ἡμέρᾳ κατὰ τὰς Γραφάς· καὶ ἀνελθόντα εἰς τοὺς οὐρανοὺς καὶ καθεζόμενον ἐκ δεξιῶν τοῦ Πατρός· καὶ πάλιν ἐρχόμενον μετὰ δόξης κρῖναι ζῶντας καὶ νεκρούς· οὗ τῆς βασιλείας οὐκ ἔσται τέλος.
title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
author: Council of Nicaea / Constantinople

s: III. The Spirit, Church, and Resurrection
en: And in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father, who together with the Father and the Son is worshipped and glorified, who spoke through the prophets. In one, holy, catholic, and apostolic Church. We confess one baptism for the remission of sins. We look forward to the resurrection of the dead and the life of the age to come. Amen.
orig: Καὶ εἰς τὸ Πνεῦμα τὸ Ἅγιον, τὸ Κύριον, τὸ ζωοποιόν, τὸ ἐκ τοῦ Πατρὸς ἐκπορευόμενον, τὸ σὺν Πατρὶ καὶ Υἱῷ συμπροσκυνούμενον καὶ συνδοξαζόμενον, τὸ λαλῆσαν διὰ τῶν προφητῶν· εἰς μίαν, ἁγίαν, καθολικὴν καὶ ἀποστολικὴν Ἐκκλησίαν· ὁμολογοῦμεν ἓν βάπτισμα εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν· προσδοκῶμεν ἀνάστασιν νεκρῶν καὶ ζωὴν τοῦ μέλλοντος αἰῶνος. Ἀμήν.
title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
author: Council of Nicaea / Constantinople

en: For those whom he foreknew, he also predestined to be conformed to the image (eikon, εἰκών) of his Son, so that he might be the firstborn among many brothers. And those whom he predestined, he also called; and those whom he called, he also justified; and those whom he justified, he also glorified.
orig: οὕς προέγνω καὶ προώρισεν συμμόρφους τῆς εἰκόνος τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ · εἰς τὸ εἶναι αὐτὸν πρωτότοκον ἐν πολλοῖς ἀδελφοῖς · οὓς δὲ προώρισεν · τούτους καὶ ἐκάλεσεν · καὶ οὓς ἐκάλεσεν · τούτους καὶ ἐδικαίωσεν · οὓς δὲ ἐδικαίωσεν · τούτους καὶ ἐδόξασεν ·
title: Romans 8:29-30: Conformed to the Image of His Son
author: Paul (attributed)

en: I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. And the life I now live in the flesh, I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.
orig: Χριστῷ συνεσταύρωμαι · ζῶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγώ · ζῆ δὲ ἐν ἐμοὶ Χριστός · ὃ δὲ νῦν ζῶ ἐν σαρκί · ἐν πίστει ζῶ τῆ τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ · τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντός με καὶ παραδόντος ἑαυτὸν ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ ·
title: Galatians 2:20: Christ Lives in Me
author: Paul (attributed)

en: He is the image (eikon, εἰκών) of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. For in him all things were created, in the heavens and on the earth, things visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities—all things have been created through him and for him. And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.
orig: ὅς ἐστιν εἰκὼν τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ἀοράτου · πρωτότοκος πάσης κτίσεως · ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ ἐκτίσθη τὰ πάντα · ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς καὶ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς · τὰ ὁρατὰ καὶ τὰ ἀόρατα · εἴτε θρόνοι εἴτε κυριότητες εἴτε ἀρχαὶ εἴτε ἐξουσίαι · τὰ πάντα δι’ αὐτοῦ καὶ εἰς αὐτὸν ἔκτισται · καὶ αὐτός ἐστιν πρὸ πάντων · καὶ τὰ πάντα ἐν αὐτῷ συνέστηκεν ·
title: Colossians 1:15-20: The Christ Hymn
author: Paul (attributed)

en: And he is the head of the body, the church. He is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, so that in all things he might have first place. For in him all the fullness (pleroma, πλήρωμα) of God was pleased to dwell, and through him to reconcile all things to himself, making peace through the blood of his cross—through him, whether things on earth or things in the heavens.
orig: καὶ αὐτός ἐστιν ἡ κεφαλὴ τοῦ σώματος τῆς ἐκκλησίας · ὅς ἐστιν ἀρχή · πρωτότοκος ἐκ τῶν νεκρῶν · ἵνα γένηται ἐν πᾶσιν αὐτὸς πρωτεύων · ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ εὐδόκησεν πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα κατοικῆσαι · καὶ δι’ αὐτοῦ ἀποκαταλλάξαι τὰ πάντα εἰς αὐτόν · εἰρηνοποιήσας διὰ τοῦ αἵματος τοῦ σταυροῦ αὐτοῦ · δι’ αὐτοῦ εἴτε τὰ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς εἴτε τὰ ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς ·
title: Colossians 1:15-20: The Christ Hymn
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Have this mind among yourselves, which was also in Christ Jesus, who, being in the form (morphe, μορφή) of God, did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of human beings. And being found in appearance as a human being, he humbled himself, becoming obedient to the point of death—even death on a cross.
orig: τοῦτο φρονεῖτε ἐν ὑμῖν ὃ καὶ ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ · ὃς ἐν μορφῆ θεοῦ ὑπάρχων · οὐχ ἁρπαγμὸν ἡγήσατο τὸ εἶναι ἴσα θεῷ · ἀλλὰ ἑαυτὸν ἐκένωσεν · μορφὴν δούλου λαβών · ἐν ὁμοιώματι ἀνθρώπων γενόμενος · καὶ σχήματι εὑρεθεὶς ὡς ἄνθρωπος · ἐταπείνωσεν ἑαυτὸν · γενόμενος ὑπήκοος μέχρι θανάτου · θανάτου δὲ σταυροῦ ·
title: Philippians 2:5-11: The Kenosis Hymn
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Therefore God also highly exalted him and gave him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.
orig: διὸ καὶ ὁ θεὸς αὐτὸν ὑπερύψωσεν · καὶ ἐχαρίσατο αὐτῷ τὸ ὄνομα τὸ ὑπὲρ πᾶν ὄνομα · ἵνα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι Ἰησοῦ πᾶν γόνυ κάμψῃ · ἐπουρανίων καὶ ἐπιγείων καὶ καταχθονίων · καὶ πᾶσα γλῶσσα ἐξομολογήσηται ὅτι κύριος Ἰησοῦς Χριστός · εἰς δόξαν θεοῦ πατρός ·
title: Philippians 2:5-11: The Kenosis Hymn
author: Paul (attributed)

en: See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God—and so we are. The reason the world does not know us is that it did not know him. Beloved, we are now children of God, and what we shall be has not yet been revealed. We know that when he appears, we shall be like him (homoioi auto, ὅμοιοι αὐτῷ), for we shall see him as he is. And everyone who has this hope in him purifies himself, just as he is pure.
orig: ἴδετε ποταπὴν ἀγάπην δέδωκεν ἡμῖν ὁ πατήρ · ἵνα τέκνα θεοῦ κληθῶμεν · καὶ ἐσμέν · διὰ τοῦτο ὁ κόσμος οὐ γινώσκει ἡμᾶς · ὅτι οὐκ ἔγνω αὐτόν · ἀγαπητοί · νῦν τέκνα θεοῦ ἐσμεν · καὶ οὔπω ἐφανερώθη τί ἐσόμεθα · οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἐὰν φανερωθῆ · ὅμοιοι αὐτῷ ἐσόμεθα · ὅτι ὀψόμεθα αὐτὸν καθώς ἐστιν · καὶ πᾶς ὁ ἔχων τὴν ἐλπίδα ταύτην ἐπ’ αὐτῷ · ἁγνίζει ἑαυτόν · καθὼς ἐκεῖνος ἁγνός ἐστιν ·
title: 1 John 3:1-3: We Shall Be Like Him
author: John the Elder (attributed)

en: And he himself gave some as apostles, some as prophets, some as evangelists, some as pastors and teachers, for the equipping of the saints for the work of ministry, for the building up of the body of Christ, until we all attain to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to a mature person, to the measure of the stature of the fullness (pleroma, πλήρωμα) of Christ.
orig: καὶ αὐτὸς ἔδωκεν τοὺς μὲν ἀποστόλους · τοὺς δὲ προφήτας · τοὺς δὲ εὐαγγελιστάς · τοὺς δὲ ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους · πρὸς τὸν καταρτισμὸν τῶν ἁγίων εἰς ἔργον διακονίας · εἰς οἰκοδομὴν τοῦ σώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ · μέχρι καταντήσωμεν οἱ πάντες εἰς τὴν ἑνότητα τῆς πίστεως καὶ τῆς ἐπιγνώσεως τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ · εἰς ἄνδρα τέλειον · εἰς μέτρον ἡλικίας τοῦ πληρώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ ·
title: Ephesians 4:11-16: The Measure of the Fullness of Christ
author: Paul (attributed)

en: So that we may no longer be infants, tossed about by waves and carried around by every wind of teaching, by the trickery of people, by craftiness in deceitful scheming. But speaking the truth in love, we are to grow up in all things into him who is the head, Christ, from whom the whole body, joined and held together by every supporting ligament, according to the working in measure of each individual part, causes the growth of the body for the building up of itself in love.
orig: ἵνα μηκέτι ὦμεν νήπιοι · κλυδωνιζόμενοι καὶ περιφερόμενοι παντὶ ἀνέμῳ τῆς διδασκαλίας · ἐν τῇ κυβείᾳ τῶν ἀνθρώπων · ἐν πανουργίᾳ πρὸς τὴν μεθοδείαν τῆς πλάνης · ἀληθεύοντες δὲ ἐν ἀγάπῃ αὐξήσωμεν εἰς αὐτὸν τὰ πάντα · ὃς ἐστιν ἡ κεφαλή · Χριστός · ἐξ οὗ πᾶν τὸ σῶμα συναρμολογούμενον καὶ συμβιβαζόμενον διὰ πάσης ἁφῆς τῆς ἐπιχορηγίας · κατ’ ἐνέργειαν ἐν μέτρῳ ἑνὸς ἑκάστου μέρους · τὴν αὔξησιν τοῦ σώματος ποιεῖται εἰς οἰκοδομὴν ἑαυτοῦ ἐν ἀγάπῃ ·
title: Ephesians 4:11-16: The Measure of the Fullness of Christ
author: Paul (attributed)

en: In many parts and in many ways God spoke long ago to the fathers through the prophets. In these last days he has spoken to us in a Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, through whom also he made the ages.
orig: πολυμερῶς καὶ πολυτρόπως πάλαι ὁ θεὸς λαλήσας τοῖς πατράσιν ἐν τοῖς προφήταις · ἐπ’ ἐσχάτου τῶν ἡμερῶν τούτων ἐλάλησεν ἡμῖν ἐν υἱῷ · ὃν ἔθηκεν κληρονόμον πάντων · δι’ οὗ καὶ ἐποίησεν τοὺς αἰῶνας ·
title: Hebrews 1:1-4: The Radiance of God's Glory
author: Author of Hebrews

en: He is the radiance (apaugasma, ἀπαύγασμα) of his glory and the exact imprint (charakter, χαρακτήρ) of his nature (hypostasis, ὑπόστασις), upholding all things by the word of his power. Having made purification for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high, having become as much superior to the angels as the name he has inherited is more excellent than theirs.
orig: ὃς ὢν ἀπαύγασμα τῆς δόξης καὶ χαρακτὴρ τῆς ὑποστάσεως αὐτοῦ · φέρων τε τὰ πάντα τῷ ῥήματι τῆς δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ · καθαρισμὸν τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ποιησάμενος ἐκάθισεν ἐν δεξιᾷ τῆς μεγαλωσύνης ἐν ὑψηλοῖς · τοσούτῳ κρείττων γενόμενος τῶν ἀγγέλων · ὅσῳ διαφορώτερον παρ’ αὐτοὺς κεκληρονόμηκεν ὄνομα ·
title: Hebrews 1:1-4: The Radiance of God's Glory
author: Author of Hebrews

en: So also is the resurrection of the dead. It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption. It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power. It is sown a natural body (soma psychikon, σῶμα ψυχικόν); it is raised a spiritual body (soma pneumatikon, σῶμα πνευματικόν). If there is a natural body, there is also a spiritual body. So also it is written: the first man Adam became a living soul; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit.
orig: οὕτως καὶ ἡ ἀνάστασις τῶν νεκρῶν · σπείρεται ἐν φθορᾷ · ἐγείρεται ἐν ἀφθαρσίᾳ · σπείρεται ἐν ἀτιμίᾳ · ἐγείρεται ἐν δόξῃ · σπείρεται ἐν ἀσθενείᾳ · ἐγείρεται ἐν δυνάμει · σπείρεται σῶμα ψυχικόν · ἐγείρεται σῶμα πνευματικόν · εἰ ἔστιν σῶμα ψυχικόν · ἔστιν καὶ πνευματικόν · οὕτως καὶ γέγραπται · ἐγένετο ὁ πρῶτος ἄνθρωπος Ἀδὰμ εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν · ὁ ἔσχατος Ἀδὰμ εἰς πνεῦμα ζωοποιοῦν ·
title: 1 Corinthians 15:42-49: The Image of the Heavenly
author: Paul (attributed)

en: But it is not the spiritual that is first, but the natural, and then the spiritual. The first man was from the earth, made of dust; the second man is from heaven. As was the man of dust, so also are those who are of the dust; and as is the man of heaven, so also are those who are of heaven. And just as we have borne the image (eikon, εἰκών) of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven.
orig: ἀλλ’ οὐ πρῶτον τὸ πνευματικόν · ἀλλὰ τὸ ψυχικόν · ἔπειτα τὸ πνευματικόν · ὁ πρῶτος ἄνθρωπος ἐκ γῆς χοϊκός · ὁ δεύτερος ἄνθρωπος ἐξ οὐρανοῦ · οἷος ὁ χοϊκός · τοιοῦτοι καὶ οἱ χοϊκοί · καὶ οἷος ὁ ἐπουράνιος · τοιοῦτοι καὶ οἱ ἐπουράνιοι · καὶ καθὼς ἐφορέσαμεν τὴν εἰκόνα τοῦ χοϊκοῦ · φορέσομεν καὶ τὴν εἰκόνα τοῦ ἐπουρανίου ·
title: 1 Corinthians 15:42-49: The Image of the Heavenly
author: Paul (attributed)

en: But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of a woman, born under the law, in order that he might redeem those under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons (huiothesia, υἱοθεσία). And because you are sons, God sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, “Abba, Father!” So you are no longer a slave but a son, and if a son, then an heir through God.
orig: ὅτε δὲ ἦλθεν τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ χρόνου · ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ · γενόμενον ἐκ γυναικός · γενόμενον ὑπὸ νόμον · ἵνα τοὺς ὑπὸ νόμον ἐξαγοράσῃ · ἵνα τὴν υἱοθεσίαν ἀπολάβωμεν · ὅτι δέ ἐστε υἱοί · ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰς καρδίας ἡμῶν · κρᾶζον · Αββα ὁ πατήρ · ὥστε οὐκέτι εἶ δοῦλος ἀλλὰ υἱός · εἰ δὲ υἱός · καὶ κληρονόμος διὰ θεοῦ ·
title: Galatians 4:4-7: Adoption as Sons
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For all who are led by the Spirit of God, these are sons of God. For you did not receive a spirit of slavery leading to fear again, but you received a spirit of adoption (huiothesia, υἱοθεσία), by which we cry, “Abba, Father!” The Spirit itself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God, and if children, then heirs—heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ (synkleronomoi Christou, συγκληρονόμοι Χριστοῦ), if indeed we suffer with him, so that we may also be glorified with him.
orig: ὅσοι γὰρ πνεύματι θεοῦ ἄγονται · οὗτοι υἱοί εἰσιν θεοῦ · οὐ γὰρ ἐλάβετε πνεῦμα δουλείας πάλιν εἰς φόβον · ἀλλὰ ἐλάβετε πνεῦμα υἱοθεσίας · ἐν ὧ κράζομεν · Αββα ὁ πατήρ · αὐτὸ τὸ πνεῦμα συμμαρτυρεῖ τῷ πνεύματι ἡμῶν · ὅτι ἐσμὲν τέκνα θεοῦ · εἰ δὲ τέκνα · καὶ κληρονόμοι · κληρονόμοι μὲν θεοῦ · συγκληρονόμοι δὲ Χριστοῦ · εἴπερ συμπάσχομεν · ἵνα καὶ συνδοξασθῶμεν ·
title: Romans 8:14-17: Heirs of God, Co-Heirs with Christ
author: Paul (attributed)

en: And Moses said to God, “Behold, I shall go to the sons of Israel and say to them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you.’ And they will ask me, ‘What is his name?’ What shall I say to them?” And God said to Moses, “I am the one who is” (ego eimi ho on, ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν). And he said, “Thus shall you say to the sons of Israel: ‘The one who is’ (ho on, ὁ ὤν) has sent me to you.” And God said again to Moses, “Thus shall you say to the sons of Israel: ‘The Lord, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.’ This is my name forever, and this is my memorial to all generations.”
orig: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς τὸν θεόν Ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ πορεύσομαι πρὸς τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ καὶ ἐρῶ πρὸς αὐτούς ὁ θεὸς τῶν πατέρων ὑμῶν ἀπέσταλκέν με πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἐρωτήσουσίν με τί ὄνομα αὐτῷ τί ἐρῶ πρὸς αὐτούς · καὶ εἶπεν ὁ θεὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν · καὶ εἶπεν οὕτως ἐρεῖς τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ὁ ὢν ἀπέσταλκέν με πρὸς ὑμᾶς · καὶ εἶπεν ὁ θεὸς πάλιν πρὸς Μωυσῆν οὕτως ἐρεῖς τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ κύριος ὁ θεὸς τῶν πατέρων ὑμῶν θεὸς Αβρααμ καὶ θεὸς Ισαακ καὶ θεὸς Ιακωβ ἀπέσταλκέν με πρὸς ὑμᾶς · τοῦτό μου ἐστιν ὄνομα αἰώνιον καὶ μνημόσυνον γενεῶν γενεαῖς ·
title: Exodus 3:13-15: I AM WHO I AM
author: Moses (attributed)

en: In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord sitting on a throne, high and lifted up, and the house was full of his glory. And seraphim stood around him; each one had six wings: with two they covered their face, and with two they covered their feet, and with two they flew. And one cried out to another and said, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory.” And the lintel was lifted up by the voice with which they cried out, and the house was filled with smoke.
orig: καὶ ἐγένετο τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ οὗ ἀπέθανεν Οζιας ὁ βασιλεύς εἶδον τὸν κύριον καθήμενον ἐπὶ θρόνου ὑψηλοῦ καὶ ἐπηρμένου καὶ πλήρης ὁ οἶκος τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ · καὶ σεραφιν εἱστήκεισαν κύκλῳ αὐτοῦ ἓξ πτέρυγες τῷ ἑνί καὶ ἓξ πτέρυγες τῷ ἑνί · καὶ ταῖς μὲν δυσὶν κατεκάλυπτον τὸ πρόσωπον καὶ ταῖς δυσὶν κατεκάλυπτον τοὺς πόδας καὶ ταῖς δυσὶν ἐπέταντο · καὶ ἐκέκραγον ἕτερος πρὸς τὸν ἕτερον καὶ ἔλεγον ἅγιος ἅγιος ἅγιος κύριος σαβαωθ πλήρης πᾶσα ἡ γῆ τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ · καὶ ἐπήρθη τὸ ὑπέρθυρον ἀπὸ τῆς φωνῆς ἧς ἐκέκραγον καὶ ὁ οἶκος ἐπλήσθη καπνοῦ ·
title: Isaiah 6:1-8: The Throne Vision
author: Isaiah (attributed)

en: And I said, “Woe is me, for I am pierced to the heart! For I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips, and my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts!” And one of the seraphim was sent to me, and in his hand he held a burning coal, which he had taken with tongs from the altar. And he touched my mouth and said, “Behold, this has touched your lips and will take away your iniquities, and will cleanse your sins.” And I heard the voice of the Lord saying, “Whom shall I send, and who will go to this people?” And I said, “Here am I; send me.”
orig: καὶ εἶπα ὦ τάλας ἐγώ ὅτι κατανένυγμαι ὅτι ἄνθρωπος ὢν καὶ ἀκάθαρτα χείλη ἔχων ἐν μέσῳ λαοῦ ἀκάθαρτα χείλη ἔχοντος ἐγὼ οἰκῶ καὶ τὸν βασιλέα κύριον σαβαωθ εἶδον τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς μου · καὶ ἀπεστάλη πρός με ἓν τῶν σεραφιν καὶ ἐν τῇ χειρὶ εἶχεν ἄνθρακα ὃν τῇ λαβίδι ἔλαβεν ἀπὸ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου · καὶ ἥψατο τοῦ στόματός μου καὶ εἶπεν ἰδοὺ ἥψατο τοῦτο τῶν χειλέων σου καὶ ἀφελεῖ τὰς ἀνομίας σου καὶ τὰς ἁμαρτίας σου περικαθαριεῖ · καὶ ἤκουσα τῆς φωνῆς κυρίου λέγοντος τίνα ἀποστείλω καὶ τίς πορεύσεται πρὸς τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον · καὶ εἶπα ἰδοὺ ἐγώ εἰμι ἀπόστειλόν με ·
title: Isaiah 6:1-8: The Throne Vision
author: Isaiah (attributed)

en: And above the firmament that was over their heads was the likeness of a throne, as the appearance of a sapphire stone; and upon the likeness of the throne was a likeness as the appearance of a man upon it above. And I saw as the appearance of amber (elektron, Ἠλεκτρον), as the appearance of fire within it round about, from the appearance of his loins and upward; and from the appearance of his loins and downward I saw as the appearance of fire, and its brightness round about, as the appearance of the bow that is in the cloud on the day of rain—so was the appearance of the brightness round about. This was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the Lord (homoioma doxes kyriou, ὁμοίωμα δόξης κυρίου). And I saw and fell upon my face, and I heard a voice speaking.
orig: καὶ ὑπεράνω τοῦ στερεώματος τοῦ ὑπὲρ κεφαλῆς αὐτῶν ὡς ὅρασις λίθου σαπφείρου ὁμοίωμα θρόνου ἐπ’ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐπὶ τοῦ ὁμοιώματος τοῦ θρόνου ὁμοίωμα ὡς εἶδος ἀνθρώπου ἄνωθεν · καὶ εἶδον ὡς ὄψιν Ἠλεκτρου ὡς ὅρασιν πυρὸς ἔσωθεν αὐτοῦ κύκλῳ ἀπὸ ὁράσεως ὀσφύος καὶ ἐπάνω καὶ ἀπὸ ὁράσεως ὀσφύος καὶ ἕως κάτω εἶδον ὡς ὅρασιν πυρὸς καὶ τὸ φέγγος αὐτοῦ κύκλῳ ὡς ὅρασις τόξου ὅταν ᾖ ἐν τῇ νεφέλῃ ἐν ἡμέρᾳ ὑετοῦ οὕτως ἡ στάσις τοῦ φέγγους κύκλῳ · αὕτη ἡ ὅρασις ὁμοιώματος δόξης κυρίου · καὶ εἶδον καὶ πίπτω ἐπὶ πρόσωπόν μου καὶ ἤκουσα φωνὴν λαλοῦντος ·
title: Ezekiel 1:26-28: The Divine Throne-Chariot
author: Ezekiel (attributed)

en: I was watching in the night visions, and behold, one like a son of man (hos huios anthropou, ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου) was coming with the clouds of heaven. And he came as far as the Ancient of Days, and he was brought before him. And to him was given the dominion and the honour and the kingdom, and all peoples, tribes, and tongues shall serve him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion that shall not pass away, and his kingdom shall not be destroyed.
orig: ἐθεώρουν ἐν ὁράματι τῆς νυκτὸς καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐπὶ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος ἦν καὶ ἕως τοῦ παλαιοῦ τῶν ἡμερῶν ἔφθασεν καὶ προσηνέχθη αὐτῷ · καὶ αὐτῷ ἐδόθη ἡ ἀρχὴ καὶ ἡ τιμὴ καὶ ἡ βασιλεία καὶ πάντες οἱ λαοί φυλαί καὶ γλῶσσαι αὐτῷ λατρεύσουσιν · ἡ ἐξουσία αὐτοῦ ἐξουσία αἰώνιος ἥτις οὐ παρελεύσεται καὶ ἡ βασιλεία αὐτοῦ οὐ διαφθαρήσεται ·
title: Daniel 7:13-14: One Like a Son of Man
author: Daniel (attributed)

en: The Lord created me (ektise me, ἔκτισέ με) as the beginning of his ways, for his works. Before the age he established me, in the beginning, before he made the earth, and before he made the depths, before the springs of waters came forth, before the mountains were settled, and before all hills, he begets me (genna me, γεννᾷ με).
orig: κύριος ἔκτισέν με ἀρχὴν ὁδῶν αὐτοῦ εἰς ἔργα αὐτοῦ · πρὸ τοῦ αἰῶνος ἐθεμελίωσέν με ἐν ἀρχῇ · πρὸ τοῦ τὴν γῆν ποιῆσαι καὶ πρὸ τοῦ τὰς ἀβύσσους ποιῆσαι πρὸ τοῦ προελθεῖν τὰς πηγὰς τῶν ὑδάτων · πρὸ τοῦ ὄρη ἑδρασθῆναι πρὸ δὲ πάντων βουνῶν γεννᾷ με ·
title: Proverbs 8:22-31: Wisdom Before Creation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: When he prepared the heaven, I was present with him, and when he marked out his own throne upon the winds, and when he made strong the clouds above, and when he secured the fountains of the earth, and when he made strong the foundations of the earth, I was beside him, fitting all things together (harmosousa, ἁρμόζουσα). I was that in which he delighted; daily I rejoiced before his face at every time, when he rejoiced at having completed the world, and rejoiced among the sons of men.
orig: ἡνίκα ἡτοίμαζεν τὸν οὐρανόν συμπαρήμην αὐτῷ καὶ ὅτε ἀφώριζεν τὸν ἑαυτοῦ θρόνον ἐπ’ ἀνέμων · καὶ ἡνίκα ἰσχυρὰ ἐποίει τὰ ἄνω νέφη καὶ ὡς ἀσφαλεῖς ἐτίθει πηγὰς τῆς ὑπ’ οὐρανόν · καὶ ὡς ἰσχυρὰ ἐποίει τὰ θεμέλια τῆς γῆς · ἤμην παρ’ αὐτῷ ἁρμόζουσα · ἐγὼ ἤμην ἧ προσέχαιρεν καθ’ ἡμέραν δὲ εὐφραινόμην ἐν προσώπῳ αὐτοῦ ἐν παντὶ καιρῷ · ὅτε εὐφραίνετο τὴν οἰκουμένην συντελέσας καὶ εὐφραίνετο ἐν υἱοῖς ἀνθρώπων ·
title: Proverbs 8:22-31: Wisdom Before Creation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: I have seen the task that God has given to the sons of men to be occupied with. He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has placed eternity (aion, αἰών) in their hearts, yet so that man cannot find out the work that God has done from the beginning to the end.
orig: εἶδον σὺν τὸν περισπασμόν ὃν ἔδωκεν ὁ θεὸς τοῖς υἱοῖς τῶν ἀνθρώπων τοῦ περισπᾶσθαι ἐν αὐτῷ · σὺν τὰ πάντα ἐποίησεν καλὰ ἐν καιρῷ αὐτοῦ · καί γε σὺν τὸν αἰῶνα ἔδωκεν ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτῶν · ὅπως μὴ εὕρῃ ὁ ἄνθρωπος τὸ ποίημα ὃ ἐποίησεν ὁ θεὸς ἀπ’ ἀρχῆς καὶ μέχρι τέλους ·
title: Ecclesiastes 3:10-11: Eternity in the Heart
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: The Word of God became man so that you too may learn from a man how a man becomes God.
orig: Λόγος ο του Θεου άνθρωπος γενόμενος, ίνα δη και συ παρα ανθρώπου μάθης πή ποτε άνθρωπος γένηται Θεός.
title: Protrepticus 1.8.4: The Word Became Man So You May Learn How Man Becomes God
author: Clement of Alexandria

en: The end is always like the beginning. As there is one end of all things, so we must understand that there is one beginning of all things, and as there is one end of many things, so from one beginning arise many differences and varieties, which in their turn are restored, through God’s goodness, through their subjection to Christ and their unity with the Holy Spirit, to one end which is like the beginning. That is to say, all those who bend the knee to the name of Jesus and thereby declare their subjection to him shall, in the restitution of all things, be gathered into one end. And God shall be all in all.
orig: Τέλος αεί τη αρχή όμοιον. Ώσπερ ουν εν πάντων τέλος, ουτως μίαν αρχήν πάντων νοείν δεί· και ως ενός τέλους πολλών όντος, ουτως απο μιας αρχής πολλαί διαφοραί και ποικιλίαι, αι πάλιν δια της χρηστότητος του Θεου αποκαθίστανται εις εν τέλος όμοιον τη αρχή· ίνα η ο Θεός τα πάντα εν πασιν.
title: De Principiis III.6.1: The End Is Like the Beginning
author: Origen of Alexandria

en: Through the Holy Spirit comes our restoration to Paradise, our ascent to the Kingdom of heaven, our adoption as sons, our freedom to call God our Father, our becoming partakers of the grace of Christ, our being called children of light, our sharing in eternal glory, and, in a word, our being brought into a state of all fullness of blessing, both in this world and in the world to come. Contemplating as in a mirror the blessings that are in store, which are promised to us through faith, we await the enjoyment of them as though they were already present, so extraordinary is the pleasure we take in them. If this is the pledge, what must the perfection be? And if the first-fruits are so great, what must the full harvest be? Through the Spirit we become like God; through the Spirit, what is beyond all desire: we become God.
orig: Δια του αγίου Πνεύματος η εις παράδεισον αποκατάστασις, η εις βασιλείαν ουρανών ανάβασις, η εις υιοθεσίαν επάνοδος, η παρρησία του καλείν Πατέρα τον Θεόν, το κοινωνον γενέσθαι της χάριτος του Χριστου, το τέκνον φωτος χρηματίζειν, το αιδίου δόξης μετέχειν· δια του Πνεύματος η προς Θεον ομοίωσις, και το υπερ πασαν επιθυμίαν, Θεον γενέσθαι.
title: On the Holy Spirit 9.23: Through the Spirit We Become God
author: Basil of Caesarea

en: He was mortal, but God. He was of the line of David, but the maker of Adam. He was clothed in flesh, but free from the things of the flesh. He had a mother, but a virgin mother. He was bound, yet he loosed. He was sold, and very cheaply — for thirty pieces of silver — yet he redeemed the world, and at great cost, for the price was his own blood. As a sheep he was led to the slaughter, but he is the Shepherd of Israel and now of the whole world. As a lamb he was silent, yet he is the Word, proclaimed by the voice of one crying in the wilderness. He was bruised and wounded, but he heals every disease and every infirmity. He was lifted up and nailed to the tree, but by the tree of life he restores us. He died, but he gives life, and by his death destroys death. He was buried, but he rose again. He descended into Hades, but he brought up souls from there. He ascended to heaven, and shall come again to judge the living and the dead. If the one set of facts makes you stumble, the other set should lift you up.
orig: Θνητός, αλλα Θεός· εκ γένους Δαβίδ, αλλα πλάστης Αδάμ· σαρκοφόρος, αλλα και ασώματος· μητρί μεν εγνωσθη, παρθένω δέ· εδέθη, αλλα και έλυσεν· επράθη, και λίαν ευτελώς, τριάκοντα αργυρίων· αλλα λυτρουται κόσμον, και μεγάλης τιμής, του ιδίου αίματος.
title: Third Theological Oration 19: On the Son and Theosis
author: Gregory of Nazianzus

en: The Godhead is purity, freedom from passion, and separation from all evil. If therefore these things are in you, God is indeed in you. If your mind is untouched by any evil, free from passion, purified from all stain, then you are blessed because of your clear-sightedness. For by looking at your own purity, you perceive the image of the divine nature as in a mirror and a reflection. And if we may use a somewhat bold expression, it is the nature of your mind that is the image of the beauty above. Just as in the case of mirrors, what appears in the material substance takes its form from the face that presents itself, so the nature that was formed after the divine is made like it, and if it looks upon the Good it is stamped with the image of the Good, while if it turns to the evil it takes upon itself the form of that which is contrary to the Good.
orig: Καθαρότης, απάθεια, κακου παντός αλλοτρίωσις η θεότης εστίν. Ει ουν ταυτα εν σοί, Θεός εν σοι πάντως εστίν. Ει ακήρατος κακου παντος η διάνοια, πάθους ελευθέρα, παντός μολυσμου καθαρεύουσα, μακάριος ει της οξυδερκίας· ότι το τοις κεκαθαρμένοις αθέατον, οιον εν κατόπτρω και εικόνι, της θείας φύσεως την εικόνα εν τη ιδία καθαρότητι εποπτεύων.
title: On the Making of Man 16: The Mirror of the Divine
author: Gregory of Nyssa

en: The human person was introduced last among beings, as a kind of natural bond mediating between the universal extremes through its own parts, and bringing into unity in itself those things that are by nature far distant from each other. Accordingly, the human person was to unite: first, the division of its own nature, as male and female, through a dispassionate relation to divine virtue, for which the distinctions of this division are in no way necessary; then, through the holiness of its life, it was to unite paradise and the inhabited earth, making of them one earth; then, by a life identical with that of the angels, it was to unite heaven and earth, making of them one creation; then, by a knowledge equal to that of the angels, it was to unite the sensible and the intelligible, making of them one world; and finally, through love, it was to unite created nature with the uncreated, showing them to be one and the same through the acquisition of grace — the whole human person wholly pervading the whole God, and becoming everything that God is, save at the level of identity of essence, and receiving to itself the whole of God.
orig: Τελευταιον των οντων εισήχθη ο ανθρωπος, οιον τις φυσικός σύνδεσμος δια των οικείων μερων πασι τοις ακροις μεσιτεύων, και εις εν αγων εν εαυτω τα πολλη τη φύσει διεστηκότα και αλλήλων απέχοντα· όλον όλω τω Θεω περιχωρήσαντα, και παν ο εστιν ο Θεός γενόμενον, χωρις της κατ’ ουσίαν ταυτότητος, και όλον εαυτω τον Θεον αντιλαβόν.
title: Ambigua 7: The Five Cosmic Divisions
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: For we are called to participate in the divine nature, as the wise Peter says, not metaphorically but truly, because the Holy Spirit plants in us a participation in himself and through himself in the divine nature. For we have been made partakers of Christ and of the Holy Spirit, and the beauty of the ineffable Godhead shines forth in our souls through the Spirit who dwells in us. And thus those who are worthy to participate in the Spirit are called gods, not by grace alone, as those who are promoted to some honour, but because they truly have God dwelling in them and with them.
orig: Κεκλήμεθα γαρ εις μετουσίαν της θείας φύσεως, καθά φησιν ο σοφός Πέτρος, ου τροπικως αλλα αληθως· ότι το αγιον Πνευμα μετουσίαν εαυτου και δι εαυτου της θείας φύσεως εγκατασπείρει ημιν· μέτοχοι γαρ Θεου και του αγίου Πνεύματος γεγόναμεν.
title: Commentary on John 1:9: Participation in the Divine Nature
author: Cyril of Alexandria

en: The flesh was deified and made God and made equal in divinity with the Word by the union with him, without either nature undergoing any change. And the flesh of the Lord was enriched with divine operations on account of its most pure union with the Word, that is, by a hypostatic union, without any loss of its natural properties. For it is not by its own operation that the flesh acts divinely, but by the Word united with it, since the Word manifests its own operation through the flesh. For a red-hot iron burns, not because it has acquired the power of burning by some process of reasoning, but because it has come to possess this power through its union with the fire. The flesh, then, was mortal in itself but life-giving through its hypostatic union with the Word. And we say in like manner that the deification of the will was not a change in its natural character but a union with the Word — its own natural will being fulfilled in his divine and omnipotent will — so that the human nature has all the riches of the divine nature.
orig: Εθεώθη η σαρξ και Θεός εγένετο και ομόθεος τω Λόγω τη προς αυτον ενώσει, ουδετέρας των φύσεων τροπήν υπομεινάσης· καθάπερ ο πεπυρωμένος σίδηρος καίει, ουχ ότι λογισμω τινι το καίειν κεκτηται, αλλα τη προς το πυρ ενώσει τουτο κεκτημένος.
title: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith III.15: The Final Synthesis
author: John of Damascus

en: Theosis is the attaining of likeness to God and union with God, so far as is possible. This is the common goal of every hierarchy: the continuous love of God and of things divine, a love that is sacredly worked out in a divine and unified manner; and before this, the complete and irrevocable turning away from what is contrary, the knowledge of beings as they truly are, the vision and understanding of sacred truth, the inspired participation in the uniform perfection and in the One himself, so far as may be, the feast of contemplation which nourishes the intellect and deifies everyone who is raised up to it.
orig: Θέωσις εστιν η προς Θεον ως εφικτον αφομοίωσίς τε και ένωσις. Κοινός δε πάσης ιεραρχίας σκοπός η προς Θεόν τε και τα θεια συνεχής αγάπη, ενθέως τε και ενιαίως ιερουργουμένη.
title: Divine Names 2.11: Theosis as Union with God
author: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite

en: God became man so that man might become God. But lest you think this means something unworthy, he adds a clarification through the Apostle: you are gods, and all of you children of the Most High. He made us gods by adopting us, not by generating us from himself. For there is only one Son of God by nature, who for our sake became the Son of Man, so that we who were sons of men by nature might become sons of God through him by grace. For he, remaining immutable, took on our nature so that in it he might take us up to himself, and, holding fast his own divinity, became a partaker of our weakness, so that we, being changed for the better, might by participating in his righteousness and immortality lose our own sinfulness and mortality, and preserve whatever good nature he had planted in us, now perfected by the greater good of his divine gift.
orig: Factus est Deus homo, ut homo fieret Deus. Sed ne quid indignum intellegas, addit per apostolum: ego dixi, dii estis, et filii Excelsi omnes. Fecit nos deos adoptando, non de se generando. Unus enim est Filius Dei natura, qui propter nos factus est filius hominis, ut nos qui eramus filii hominum natura, per illum efficeremur filii Dei gratia. Ille enim incommutabilis manens, naturam nostram suscepit, ut in ea nos susciperet ad se; et tenens suam divinitatem, particeps factus est nostrae infirmitatis, ut nos in melius commutati, quod peccatum et mortalitas nostra est, eius iustitiae et immortalitatis participatione amitteremus, et quod boni in natura nostra ille plantaverat, bono maiore divini muneris perfectum servaremus.
title: Sermon 166.4: The Western Theosis with Qualifications
author: Augustine of Hippo

en: Jesus said: If those who lead you say to you, “See, the kingdom is in the sky,” then the birds of the sky will precede you. If they say to you, “It is in the sea,” then the fish will precede you. Rather, the kingdom is within you and outside you. When you come to know yourselves, then you will be known, and you will realize that you are the children of the living Father. But if you do not know yourselves, then you dwell in poverty, and you are that poverty.
orig: Ⲑⲁⲟⲉ ⲓⲉ · ⲣⲉⲓⲁⲓⲉⲥ ⲓⲉⲓ · λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐὰν οἱ ὡγούμενοι λέγωσιν ὑμῖν · ἤ βασιλεία ἐντὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν καὶ ἐκτὸς ὑμῶν
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said to them: When you make the two one, and when you make the inside like the outside and the outside like the inside, and the above like the below, and when you make the male and the female one and the same, so that the male not be male nor the female female—then you will enter the kingdom.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ὅταν ποιήσητε τὰ δύο ἕν · καὶ τὸ ἔσωθεν ὡς τὸ ἔξωθεν · καὶ τὸ ἄνω ὡς τὸ κάτω
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: If they say to you, “Where did you come from?” say to them, “We came from the light, the place where the light came into being on its own accord and established itself and became manifest through their image.” If they say to you, “Is it you?” say, “We are its children, and we are the elect of the living Father.” If they ask you, “What is the sign of your Father in you?” say to them, “It is movement and repose.”
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐὰν λέγωσιν ὑμῖν · πόθεν ἐλήλυθατε · λέγετε αὐτοῖς · ἐκ τοῦ φωτὸς ἐλήλυθαμεν
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: I am the light that is over all things. I am all. From me all came forth, and to me all attained. Split a piece of wood: I am there. Lift up the stone, and you will find me there.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ φῶς τὸ ἐπί πᾶσιν · ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ πᾶν · σχίσον ξύλον · ἐγώ ἐκεῖ εἰμι
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: He who will drink from my mouth will become like me. I myself shall become he, and the things that are hidden will be revealed to him.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ὁ πίνων ἐκ τοῦ στόματός μου · γενήσεται ὡς ἐγώ · καὶ ἐγώ αὐτὸς γενήσομαι
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: It is not possible for anyone to see anything of the things that actually exist unless he becomes like them. This is not the way with man in the world: he sees the sun without being a sun; and he sees the heaven and the earth and all other things, but he is not these things. This is quite in keeping with the truth. But you saw something of that place, and you became those things. You saw the Spirit, you became spirit. You saw Christ, you became Christ. You saw the Father, you shall become Father. So in this place you see everything and do not see yourself, but in that place you do see yourself—and what you see you shall become.
orig: οὐ δύνατόν ἐστιν οὐδένα ιἰδεῖν τι τῶν πραγμάτων τῶν ὑπαρχόντων · ἐὰν μὴ γένηται ὅμοιος αὐτοῖς · τὸ πνεῦμα εἶδες · πνεῦμα ἐγένου · τὸν Χριστὸν εἶδες · Χριστὸς ἐγένου
title: Gospel of Philip: You Saw Christ, You Became Christ
author: Valentinus

en: The chrism is superior to baptism, for it is from the word “chrism” that we have been called “Christians,” certainly not because of the word “baptism.” And it is because of the chrism that “the Christ” has his name. For the Father anointed the Son, and the Son anointed the apostles, and the apostles anointed us. He who has been anointed possesses everything. He possesses the resurrection, the light, the cross, the Holy Spirit. The Father gave him this in the bridal chamber; he merely accepted the gift.
orig: τὸ χρῖσμα ὑπερέχει τοῦ βαπτίσματος · ἐκ γὰρ τοῦ χρίσματος · Χριστιανοὶ ἐκλήθημεν
title: Gospel of Philip: You Saw Christ, You Became Christ
author: Valentinus

en:
The Monad is a monarchy with nothing above it. It is he who exists as God and Father of everything, the invisible one who is above everything, who exists as incorruption, dwelling in the pure light into which no eye can look. He is the invisible Spirit, of whom it is not right to think of him as a god, or something similar. For he is more than a god, since there is nothing above him, for no one lords it over him. He does not exist in something inferior to him, since everything exists in him. It is he who establishes himself. He is eternal, since he does not need anything. He is total perfection. A being can have a relationship with a being, but the Invisible Spirit is not among such things. He is at rest in silence, he who is before all things.
        And his thought (Ennoia) performed a deed, and she came forth—she who had appeared before him in the shine of his light. She is the first power, she who was before all of them, and who came forth from his mind: she is the forethought (Pronoia) of the All—her light shines like his light—the perfect power, the image of the invisible, virginal Spirit. She became the womb of everything, for it is she who is prior to them all: the Mother-Father, the first Human, the Holy Spirit, the triple-male, the triple-powered, the triple-named androgynous one. And the eternal aeon among the invisible ones: the first to come forth—Barbelo.
orig: τὸ ἀόρατον πνεῦμα · ἡ μονάς ἐστιν μοναρχία ἧς οὐδὲν ὑπεράνω ἐστιν · καὶ ἡ ἔννοια αὐτοῦ εἰργάσατο ἔργον · καὶ προῆλθεν · ἡ Βαρβηλώ
title: Apocryphon of John: The Barbelo Cosmogony
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en:
And the power of the Mother went out of Yaltabaoth into the natural body that they had fashioned after the image of the one who exists from the beginning. The body moved and gained strength, and it was luminous. And in that moment the rest of the powers became jealous, because he had come into being through all of them—and they had given their power to the man—and his intelligence was greater than that of those who had made him, and greater than that of the chief archon. And when they recognized that he was luminous, and that he could think better than they, and that he was free from wickedness, they took him and threw him into the lowest region of all matter.
        But the blessed one, the Mother-Father, the beneficent and merciful one, had mercy on the power of the Mother that had been brought forth out of the chief archon. And he sent, through his beneficent Spirit and his great mercy, a helper to Adam—luminous Epinoia, she who is from him, who was called Life (Zoe). And she assists the whole creature, by toiling with him and restoring him to his fullness, and by teaching him about the descent of his seed, and teaching him about the way of ascent, which is the way he came down.
orig: καὶ ἡ δύναμις τῆς μητρὸς ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τοῦ Ἰαλδαβαώθ · εἰς τὸ σῶμα τὸ φυσικόν · καὶ ἐκίνησεν καὶ ἔλαβεν ἰσχύν · καὶ ἐφώτισεν
title: Apocryphon of John: The Barbelo Cosmogony
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

s: Ignorance and the Origin of the World (16.31–17.36)
en: Since the deficiency came about because the Father was not known, therefore when the Father is known, from that moment on the deficiency will no longer exist. As in the case of a person’s ignorance—when he comes to have knowledge, his ignorance vanishes of itself; as darkness vanishes when light appears—so also the deficiency vanishes in the perfection. So from that moment on, the form is no longer apparent, but it will vanish in the fusion of unity, for now their works lie scattered. In time, unity will perfect the spaces. It is within unity that each one will attain himself; within knowledge he will purify himself from multiplicity into unity, consuming matter within himself like fire, and darkness by light, death by life.
orig: ἐπεὶ τὸ ὑστέρημα ἐγένετο ἐπεὶ ὁ πατὴρ οὐκ ἐγινώσκετο · ὅταν οὖν ὁ πατὴρ γινώσκηται · τὸ ὑστέρημα οὐκέτι ἔσται
title: Gospel of Truth: Knowledge as Return to the Father
author: Valentinus

s: Knowledge and Return to the Father (21.3–22.20)
en:
He discovered them in himself, and they discovered him in themselves—the incomprehensible, inconceivable one, the Father, the perfect one, the one who made all things. Within him are all things, and all things have need of him. Although he retained their perfection within himself, which he did not give to the all, the Father was not jealous. What jealousy indeed could there be between himself and his members? For if the aeon had thus received their perfection, they could not have come to the Father. He retains within himself their perfection, granting it to them as a return to him, and a knowledge that is uniquely his. It is he who fashioned the all, and the all is within him, and the all had need of him.
        As in the case of a person whom some do not know: he wishes them to know him and to love him—for what did the all have need of, if not the knowledge of the Father?—so he became a guide, restful and at leisure. In the middle of a school he came and spoke the word as a teacher. Those who were wise in their own estimation came to test him. But he confounded them, because they were foolish. They hated him because they were not truly wise.
orig: εὗρεν αὐτοὺς ἐν ἑαυτῷ · καὶ αὐτοὶ εὗρον αὐτὸν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς · τὸν ἀκατάληπτον · τὸν ἀνεννόητον · τὸν πατέρα · τὸν τέλειον
title: Gospel of Truth: Knowledge as Return to the Father
author: Valentinus

en:
The Father exists in the Undivided One. He is the one in whom the Totality dwells and whom the Totality lacks. He projected the Totalities, he who exists within himself, whom the Totality surrounds. It is the Father in whom the root of the Totality is established. He gave them their constitutions. He was not himself the one who brought himself forth. Each one of the Totalities is a thought, and a form, and a root.
        Silence is his companion. He it is who projected the Totality from himself—that is, the eternal ones—he projected them as roots and as a spring, as fruits. They are offspring of his thought and of his will. He is the one from whom all things proceed, the one whom they all lack, since he retained within himself what they needed, what he did not give to the Totalities—the knowledge of the Father. He is the one who brought the Totalities into existence, and they knew neither where they came from nor whither they were going.
orig: ὁ πατὴρ ὑπάρχει ἐν τῷ ἀμερίστῳ · αὐτός ἐστιν ἐν ἷ τὸ πλήρωμα κατοικεῖ · καὶ ὃν τὸ πλήρωμα ὑστερεῖ · ἡ σιγὴ σύνεστιν αὐτῷ
title: A Valentinian Exposition: The Pleroma and the Fall
author: Valentinus

en:
Sophia, who is called Achamoth, had conceived a desire without the consent of her consort. And since she had no partner, her product was imperfect—for it was fashioned without her consort. And it did not resemble its mother, for it was a different form. When she saw the consequence of her desire, it had changed into the form of a serpent and a lion—with eyes that flash with fire.
        She cast it away from herself, outside that place, so that none of the immortals might see it. For she had created it in ignorance. And she surrounded it with a luminous cloud and placed a throne in the middle of the cloud, so that no one might see it except the Holy Spirit, who is called the Mother of the Living. And she named it Yaltabaoth.
        But the restoration will come when the Totality reveals itself in the one who is the Son. That is the restoration: the return to what is from the beginning. The one who has knowledge is from above. He is called to what is above. The one who has heard is called to turn himself and to ascend to the Father and to receive the Totalities—the restoration—and to discover himself there, in the place of rest.
orig: ἡ Σοφία ἡ καλουμένη Ἀχαμώθ · ἐνεθυμήθη ἐπιθυμίαν ἄνευ τῆς συναινέσεως τοῦ συζύγου αὐτῆς · ἡ δὲ ἀποκατάστασις ἔσται · ὅταν τὸ πλήρωμα φανερωθῇ
title: A Valentinian Exposition: The Pleroma and the Fall
author: Valentinus

en:
In those days, in those far-off days, in those nights, in those far-off nights, in those years, in those far-off years—in those ancient days, when the things that were needful had been brought into being, in those ancient days, when the things that were needful had been given their proper nourishment—when bread had been baked in the shrines of the land, and bread had been tasted in the houses of the land—Shuruppak gave instructions to his son. Shuruppak, the son of Ubar-Tutu, gave instructions to his son Ziusudra:
        “My son, I would give you instruction. Take my instruction.”

title: Instructions of Shuruppak: The Oldest Wisdom Text
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en:
“Do not buy a donkey that brays. It will split your very yoke.
        Do not locate a field on a road. Do not place your house near a public square.
        Do not vouch for someone. That man will have a hold on you.
        Do not curse with powerful means. The curse will recoil upon you.
        Do not speak arrogantly to your mother. That causes hatred for you.
        Do not question the words of your mother and your personal god.
        The elder brother is indeed like a father. The elder sister is indeed like a mother. Listen to your elder brother. Listen to your elder sister.
        Do not pass judgment when you drink beer.
        You should not steal anything. You should not kill yourself with a knife.
        Do not laugh with or sit alone in a room with a girl who is married.”

en:
“The night is long. Think upon it.
        My son, you should heed my instructions. Ziusudra, you should heed my instructions. The instructions of an old man are precious. May Utu let you attain them.”
        These are the instructions of Shuruppak, the son of Ubar-Tutu.

title: Instructions of Shuruppak: The Oldest Wisdom Text
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

s: Tablet I, lines 1–20: The Primordial Beginning
en:
When on high the heavens had not yet been named, and below the earth had not yet been called by name—when primordial Apsu, their begetter, and Mummu-Tiamat, she who bore them all, were mingling their waters as one, when no marsh had formed and no island could be found, when no god at all had appeared, had not been named, no destinies decreed—then were the gods created within them.
        Lahmu and Lahamu emerged, were called by name. Before they had grown, before they had matured, Anshar and Kishar were created, surpassing them. They lived for many days, adding years to years. Anu was their firstborn son, the rival of his fathers. Anshar made his firstborn Anu his equal. And Anu begat Nudimmud in his likeness.
        Nudimmud it was who was the master of his fathers: broad of understanding, wise, mighty in strength, far mightier than his grandfather Anshar. He had no rival among the gods, his brethren.

s: Tablet VI, lines 1–38: Creation of Humanity from Divine Blood
en:
When Marduk heard the speech of the gods, his heart moved him to create artful things. He opened his mouth and spoke to Ea, conveying the plan he had conceived in his heart:
        “I will bind together blood and cause bones to be. I will establish a savage, ‘Man’ shall be his name. Truly, savage-man I will create. He shall be charged with the service of the gods, that they might be at rest.”
        Ea answered him, speaking a word to him, telling him a plan for the relief of the gods:
        “Let one of the brothers be given up. Let him perish that mankind may be fashioned. Let the great gods assemble and let the guilty one be given up, that they may be confirmed.”
        Marduk assembled the great gods. Graciously he gave instruction, issued a command. The gods paid heed to his utterance. The king addressed a word to the Anunnaki:
        “If your earlier declaration was true, now declare the truth on oath by me! Who was it who contrived the uprising and made Tiamat rebel, and joined battle? Let him who contrived the uprising be given up, that I may lay his punishment upon him. You shall dwell in peace.”
        The great gods answered him, the king of the gods of heaven and earth:
        “It was Qingu who contrived the uprising and made Tiamat rebel, and joined battle.”
        They bound him, holding him before Ea. They imposed the punishment on him and severed his blood-vessels. From his blood they created mankind. Ea imposed the service of the gods upon them and set the gods free.

en:
Words spoken by the Osiris N when he arrives at this Hall of Two Truths, that he may be purified of all the evil that he has done, and that he may behold the faces of the gods:
        “Hail to you, great god, lord of the Two Truths! I have come before you, my lord. I have been brought that I might see your beauty. I know you. I know the names of the forty-two gods who are with you in this Hall of Two Truths, who live on those who store up evil, who swallow their blood on that day of the reckoning of characters before Wennefer.
        Behold, I have come to you. I have brought you truth. I have driven out falsehood for you. I have not done evil against any person. I have not mistreated cattle. I have not committed wrong in the Place of Truth.”

en:
“I have not done falsehood against any person.
I have not impoverished my associates.
I have not done wrong in the Place of Truth.
I have not learned that which is not.
I have not done any harmful thing.
I have not made the beginning of each day with demands in excess of what was due to me.
I have not brought my name forward for honors.
I have not deprived the orphan of his property.
I have not done what the gods detest.
I have not caused a servant to be mistreated by his master.
I have not caused anyone to suffer.
I have not caused anyone to go hungry.
I have not caused anyone to weep.
I have not killed.
I have not ordered anyone to kill.
I have not caused suffering to any person.
I have not diminished the offerings in the temples.
I have not debased the bread of the gods.
I have not taken the milk from the mouths of children.
I have not driven cattle from their pasture.
I have not snared the birds of the gods.
I have not dammed water in its season.
I have not built a dam against running water.
I have not quenched a fire in its time.
I have not neglected the days of meat-offerings.
I am pure. I am pure. I am pure. I am pure.”

s: The Prologue: Divine Mandate of Kingship
en:
When the supreme god Anu, king of the Anunnaki, and Enlil, lord of heaven and earth, who determines the destinies of the land, allotted the divine lordship over all peoples to Marduk, the firstborn son of Ea, made him great among the Igigi gods, called Babylon by its exalted name, made it supreme in the four quarters of the world, and established for him within it an everlasting kingship whose foundations are as firm as heaven and earth—
        at that time, Anu and Enlil named me, Hammurabi, the devout prince who fears the gods, to cause justice to prevail in the land, to destroy the wicked and the evil, that the strong should not oppress the weak, to rise like the sun over the black-headed people, and to light up the land.
        I am Hammurabi, the shepherd called by Enlil. I am he who heaps up abundance and plenty, who makes everything perfect for the city of Nippur. I am the devout provider of Ekur. I am the capable king who restored Eridu to its rightful place, who purified the rites of Eabzu.
        I am the storm that rages over the four quarters of the world, who magnifies the fame of Babylon, who gladdens the heart of Marduk his lord, who daily attends upon Esagila. I am the descendant of royalty, whom the god Sin created, who enriched the city of Ur, the pious one who brings abundance to Ekishnugal.
        I am the wise king, the obedient one, who heeds the words of Shamash. I am the mighty one who has laid the foundations of Sippar, who clothed with green the shrines of Aya, who made the temple of Ebabbar rise like a mountain in heaven.
        The great gods have called me. I am the shepherd who brings peace, whose sceptre is just. My benevolent shade is spread over my city. In my bosom I carry the peoples of Sumer and Akkad. Under my protection they have prospered. I govern them in peace. I shelter them in my wisdom.

en:
Splendid you rise in the horizon of heaven, O living Aten, creator of life! When you have dawned in the eastern horizon, you fill every land with your beauty. You are beautiful, great, radiant, high above every land. Your rays embrace the lands to the limit of all that you have made.
        You are Re: you have reached their limits. You subdue them for your beloved son. Though you are far away, your rays are on earth. Though you are in the faces of men, your movements are unseen.
        When you set in the western horizon, the land is in darkness, in the manner of death. They sleep in their chambers, their heads wrapped up, and no eye sees another. All their goods that are under their heads could be stolen, and they would not know it. Every lion comes out of its den. All the serpents bite. Darkness is a shroud, and the earth is in stillness, for he who made them rests in his horizon.
        But when the earth brightens, and you rise in the horizon and shine as the Aten by day, you drive away the darkness and offer your rays. The Two Lands are in festivity. People awake and stand on their feet, for you have raised them up. Their bodies are washed. They take their garments. Their arms are raised in praise at your appearance. The entire land goes about its work.

title: Great Hymn to the Aten: The Earliest Monotheism
author: Akhenaten (attributed)

en:
How manifold are your works! They are hidden from the face of man, O sole god, like whom there is no other! You made the earth as you wished, you alone: all peoples, herds, and flocks. All that is on earth, that walks on legs, that rises up and flies on wings.
        The foreign countries—Syria and Kush—and the land of Egypt: you set every man in his place, you make their necessities. Each one has his food, and his lifetime is reckoned. Their tongues are separate in speech, and their natures likewise. Their skins are different, for you have distinguished the foreign peoples.
        You make the Nile in the underworld, and you bring it forth as you desire, to give life to the people, inasmuch as you made them for yourself, lord of all of them, who wearies himself on their behalf. O lord of every land, who rises for them, O Aten of the day, great in majesty!
        All distant foreign countries, you make their life also. You have placed a Nile in heaven, that it may descend for them and make floods upon the mountains, like the great green sea, to water their fields among their towns.
        How effective they are, your plans, O lord of eternity! The Nile in heaven, it is your gift to the foreign peoples and to the beasts of every desert that go upon their feet. But the true Nile comes from the underworld for Egypt.
        Your rays suckle every field. When you rise, they live and grow for you. You make the seasons to nourish all that you have made: winter to cool them, and the heat of summer so they may taste you.
        You are in my heart, and there is no other who knows you except your son Akhenaten. You have made him wise in your plans and in your power. The world came into being by your hand, as you made them. When you have risen, they live. When you set, they die. You yourself are lifetime. One lives by you.
